summary: Creates a shared framework for designing and scaling inclusive, impactful experiential learning across curriculum and co-curriculum, and sets a future student entitlement ambition.
detailSummary:
King’s will coalesce, codify and advance the considerable expertise and innovative experiential practice already present across the institution, creating a mobilisable framework for experiential learning across curriculum and co-curriculum. Developed through the Experiential Learning Strategic Community of Practice, the framework will support existing innovators to push practice further while making strong approaches more visible, reusable and consistently supported.

It will articulate a distinctive King’s approach, informed by Dewey’s tradition of learning through experience and Biesta’s conception of world-centred education. Employability and graduate futures will be the principal strategic drivers, while recognising the wider value of bringing students into meaningful contact with disciplines, organisations, communities and challenges that require them to exercise agency, judgement and purpose. This also provides an important response to an AI-enabled world in which authentic action, collaboration and applied judgement are increasingly valuable.

The framework will translate this theoretical basis into practical institutional architecture: a typology of experiential learning; principles for learning design, reflection and experientially aligned assessment; reusable delivery models; and clear expectations for inclusion, support, quality and assurance. It will support different disciplines and levels of granularity, from individual activities and assessments to curriculum components, modules and wider co-curricular experiences.

By establishing common ways of working, the framework will create the conditions for experiential learning to grow efficiently, inclusively and with demonstrable impact. It will reduce repeated local redesign, align institutional support and investment around shared approaches, inform downstream operating and opportunity-growth work, and establish an ambition for the experiential learning students should ultimately expect through a King’s education. Through scholarship, institutional practice and external engagement, King’s will seek to lead thought and practice in a field rapidly converging around an insufficiently interrogated, placement-led consensus.

caseForChange.problem:
Higher education is increasingly embracing experiential learning, but often in forms shaped more by headline commitments than by educational design. It is frequently reduced to work placements and justified almost entirely through employability, overlooking its wider potential to build student agency, judgement, purpose, belonging and a deeper relationship with discipline.

Without a coherent design framework, institutions risk creating significant activity and expense without assurance of quality, inclusivity or impact. Placement-led approaches are difficult to scale equitably, do not always meet students’ developmental needs and can prioritise host requirements over learning. King’s has substantial expertise and innovative practice in this area, but it remains distributed and inconsistently codified, limiting the University’s ability to extend what works, assure quality and develop a distinctive institutional approach.
caseForChange.opportunity:
As the sector reaches for highly visible but often reductive responses, King’s has an opportunity to lead in both thought and practice. It can establish a richer and more educationally rigorous model of experiential learning: one that begins with the development students need, embraces a wider repertoire of experiences and assessments, and connects employability with agency, disciplinary identity, purpose and meaningful engagement with the world.

By coalescing existing expertise, codifying strong practice and enabling its innovators to push further, King’s can create the shared architecture needed for efficient and inclusive growth. Common principles, reusable models and clear quality expectations would reduce duplication, improve investment choices and allow provision to expand without sacrificing educational purpose or impact. This would make experiential learning a defining feature of a King’s education and establish a distinctive external voice in the field.
caseForChange.whyNow:
Experiential learning is moving rapidly to the centre of institutional promises about employability, educational value and graduate outcomes. Decisions made now will shape whether this agenda develops as a coherent educational proposition or settles into a narrow race for visible volumes of activity.

Artificial intelligence adds further urgency by increasing the importance of forms of learning through which students exercise judgement, respond to uncertainty, collaborate, experiment and apply disciplinary knowledge in authentic contexts. At the same time, King’s now has both a growing institutional appetite for experiential learning and a route, through the Strategic Community of Practice, to bring together the expertise required to shape it. Establishing the framework before significant expansion will allow future investment and growth to be designed around quality, inclusion and impact from the outset.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s will move from distributed, locally defined and inconsistently supported practice to a coherent institutional approach to experiential learning across curriculum and co-curriculum. A living framework will articulate the University’s theoretical basis, typology, design and assessment principles, delivery models, quality expectations and future student entitlement.

The framework will provide a practical reference point for educators, programme teams and professional services, while setting the design requirements for the operating model, assurance arrangements and opportunity-growth work delivered elsewhere in the programme. It will enable King’s to develop provision more consistently, efficiently and inclusively, while sustaining an evolving body of scholarship and practice through which the University can influence the wider sector.
